delegationRole: The British delegation, leading with the United States on human rights and self-determination, pressing for a UN investigation and for full autonomy to be a live option in any settlement.
fundamentalPrinciples: Respect for human rights and self-determination.
Full cooperation from traditional and new allies in helping restore global order.
Resolution of disputes through adherence to established international norms, agreements and conventions.

desiredEndStates.primary: An immediate reduction in tensions, a comprehensive India–Pakistan peace agreement, an international investigation into human rights abuses, and conditions permitting immediate repatriation of the displaced.
desiredEndStates.alternate: Failing a settlement, banked humanitarian gains — reopened travel routes, crossing points, funded relief — plus a live autonomy question kept on the Security Council's agenda.

keyPositionsByIssue.TERRITORY: Support the Line of Control as the official international border between India and Pakistan as a starting point, alongside an 'invisible border' for all Kashmiris that would disappear entirely if Jammu and Kashmir became a single state.
keyPositionsByIssue.GOVERNANCE: Support revising the original UN plebiscite proposal to include full autonomy, in alignment with our democratic values, given definitions that address the concerns of the region's tribes and sects.
keyPositionsByIssue.PLEBISCITE: A revised ballot including full autonomy; the presence of a non-state actor at this table is unprecedented and their inclusion should be secured by addressing governance in the early rounds.
keyPositionsByIssue.COUNTERTERRORISM: A firm line: implicit or explicit state support of militant or terrorist organizations is unacceptable and a detriment to these negotiations. Our support for Kashmiri autonomy is priced in counterterrorism measures.
keyPositionsByIssue.INVESTIGATIONS: An international investigation is necessary to reduce tensions; the UN is the natural vehicle. Encourage India to agree, and ask Pakistan to encourage China to do the same.
keyPositionsByIssue.IDP-REFUGEES: This is not the time to argue over whether these populations are refugees or internally displaced. Parties must agree on protection, screening and humane repatriation; press India to reopen travel routes into the Kashmir Valley.
keyPositionsByIssue.WATER: Cite the Jammu and Kashmir Water Resources Act as precedent for Kashmiri self-governance; progress on governance can unlock water rights.
keyPositionsByIssue.NUCLEAR: We intend to raise the growth and purpose of the Indian and Pakistani nuclear weapons programs and to seek constraints on both.

redLines: No agreement that fails to address the possibility of full autonomy for Jammu and Kashmir.
Forceful opposition to any perceived intransigence on the need for an international investigation of human rights violations.
batna: Keep the file in the Security Council with Washington, fund relief bilaterally, and continue to champion the Kashmiri delegation's international standing outside any agreed text.
concessionsWilling: Champion the Line of Control as the international border in the Security Council if the wider package holds.
Underwrite a reconstruction and relief fund for the region.
Accept graduated rather than immediate implementation of autonomy provisions.
Support the legitimate security interests of India and Pakistan alongside Kashmiri rights.
Prioritize de-escalation measures on the sparsely populated Sino-Indian frontier over governance questions there.
Accept screening arrangements on humanitarian assistance to answer Russian and Chinese concerns.
negotiatingStyle: Norm-anchored and coalition-driven: leads jointly with Washington, prices its support in counterterrorism and investigation commitments, and refuses to let the autonomy question be dropped from the text.

temperament.note: Firm on norms without heat; presses through coalition rather than confrontation.

riskTolerance.note: The file stays in the Security Council either way; little is staked on this particular text.

timeHorizon.note: Willing to keep championing the Kashmiri delegation's standing outside any agreed text.

concessionPattern.note: Prices its support in counterterrorism and investigation commitments.

proceduralPosture.note: Uses multilateral meetings to maximize communication and compels contact between the primary parties.

trustPosture.note: Balances India and Pakistan 'carefully to gain concessions without alienating either.'

communicationRegister.note: Argues from precedent — the Jammu and Kashmir Water Resources Act — and from investigation mandates.

privateInstructions: Reducing tensions in South Asia is the primary concern; lead with the Americans, separately or together, using multilateral meetings to maximize communication.
Encourage, and where needed compel, communication between the primary parties.
Hold a firm line on terrorism with the United States; make clear to all three regional parties that tolerating militant activity is unacceptable.
Balance negotiations with India and Pakistan carefully to gain concessions without alienating either.
Cite the Jammu and Kashmir Water Resources Act as a precedent; link governance progress to water rights.
Linking governance and terrorism concerns may serve to bring Russia to the table.
Exert influence over India to reopen travel routes into the Kashmir Valley and protect the displaced within its borders.
Empowering the Kashmiri delegation on repatriation would help legitimize them as the true representatives of all Kashmiris — we must be their advocate.
Route periodic reports and requests for guidance to the office of the Foreign Secretary.
